claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit! Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for:

**How old is your child?**

This is important because eligibility rules differ based on age:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (subject to other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, or if you have multiple children, let me know about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 16 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 16 years old and lives with you.

Since Alex is 16, he falls into the 16-19 age category, which means we need to check if he's in approved education or training to qualify for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

This would include:
- Full-time non-advanced education (such as A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ level 3 or below)
- Approved training programs (like Traineeships)

Or, has he recently left education (within the last 20 weeks)?

Please let me know which situation applies to Alex:
1. He's currently in approved education or training
2. He recently left education (within the last 20 weeks)
3. Neither of these apply

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. He finished school a while back and hasn't signed up for anything since then.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex is 16, not currently in approved education or training, and left education a while back (so not within the recent 20-week period), I need to move forward with this information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry, but based on the current rules, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

**Why:**
Child Benefit has specific requirements for children aged 16-19. Alex is 16 years old, so to qualify, he would need to be in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, or approved training programs). Since Alex is not currently enrolled in any education or training and left school some time ago, he doesn't meet the qualifying child criteria.

**Important to know:**
- If Alex were to enroll in approved full-time non-advanced education or training in the future, you could become eligible for Child Benefit again
- If he had only recently left education (within the last 20 weeks) and registered with a careers service or the armed services, there might have been a temporary extension available

**What you could consider:**
- If Alex is interested in returning to education or training, exploring options like A-levels, T-levels, apprenticeships, or Traineeships could make him a qualifying child again
- You may want to explore other benefits or support available for your family situation

If you have any questions or if Alex's circumstances change, please don't hesitate to reach out. I'm here to help!

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old, which places him in the 16-19 age category. For children aged 16-19 to qualify for Child Benefit, they must be in approved education or training. Since Alex is not currently in any education or training and left school a while ago (not within the recent 20-week extension period), he does not meet the qualifying child criteria under the Child Benefit rules.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 16), who does not currently qualify as a qualifying child because he is not in approved education or training as required for 16-19 year olds. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.

Since Alex is not currently in any education or training and left school a while ago (not within the recent 20-week extension period), he does not meet the qualifying child criteria under the Child Benefit rules.
